chunk_summary: Guidelines for pediatric parenteral nutrition emphasize tailored electrolyte and mineral dosing based on maintenance needs, measured losses, and organ function, with specific ranges for calcium, phosphorus, and magnesium across developmental stages.
learning_objectives: Understand the principles of pediatric parenteral nutrition electrolyte dosing based on organ function and measured losses.
Learn the recommended daily ranges for calcium, phosphorus, and magnesium in preterm and term infants.
Recognize the risks of refeeding-like hypophosphatemia in early preterm parenteral nutrition and strategies to mitigate it.
key_facts: Sodium: 1–3 mmol/kg/day; Potassium: 1–3 mmol/kg/day; Chloride: 1–3 mmol/kg/day, titrated to serum electrolytes and losses.
Preterm infants (first days): Calcium 0.8–2.0 mmol/kg/day, Phosphorus 1.0–2.0 mmol/kg/day, Magnesium 0.1–0.2 mmol/kg/day.
Growing premature infants: Calcium and Phosphorus 1.6–3.5 mmol/kg/day each, Magnesium 0.2–0.3 mmol/kg/day.
Term infants (up to 6 months): Calcium 0.8–1.5 mmol/kg/day, Phosphorus 0.7–1.3 mmol/kg/day, Magnesium 0.1–0.2 mmol/kg/day.
Use a molar calcium-to-phosphate ratio of 0.75:1 to 1:1 (often 0.8–1.0 early) to prevent hypophosphatemia during macronutrient escalation.
reference_quote: Prescribe pediatric PN electrolytes as 'maintenance needs plus measured losses, constrained by organ function,' not as a fixed recipe.
document_key_topics: Daily calcium, phosphorus, and magnesium requirements
